title: SPC Cases on Domestic Violence
text:
On November 21, 2025, The Supreme People’s Court  (SPC)  released a series of cases illustrating principles in cases related to domestic violence . These cases are intended to serve as both public legal education and guidance to lower courts.  The release shows a generally positive direction, and was interesting in that the cases were presented as showing compliance with international legal norms.
Case 1: Psychological Abuse Leading To Suicide
FACTS:
Couple lived together from 9/2018 to 10/2019.
1/2019, M became increasingly argumentative about F’s sexual history, constantly and cruelly berating her.
6/2019, F cut her wrists after an argument.
8/2019, F attempted suicide by taking pills, but was revived.
10/2019, F fled to a hotel after an argument with M, ordered pills online, and ended her own life.
Verdict:  Sentenced to 3 years, 2 months prison.
Legal Principles:
Cohabitating partners are “family members” covered by the crime of familial abuse.   Case materials discuss that romantic cohabitation includes the unique features of family violence: occuring in private, difficult to remove oneself and avoid escalation.
Article 260:  [Criminal Abuse]  Where the circumstances are heinous, abuse of  family members  is to be given a sentence of up to two years imprisonment, criminal detention, or controlled release.
Violations of the crime in the preceding paragraph that cause serious injury or death to the defendant are given a sentence of between 2 and 7 years imprisonment.
The crime in the first paragraph is handled only upon complaint, except where the victim lacks the capacity to make a complaint, or is unable to make a complaint due to coercion or intimidation.
The  Domestic Violence Law  explicitly extends to cover cohabitation, but this case demonstrates how that should impact the application of the  Criminal Law  in domestic violence cases.
2.  Sustained humiliation and degradation, causing psychological torment, can be addressed as Criminal Familial ABUSE when the circumstances are vile.  This follows an interpretation of domestic violence offenses released by the SPC in 2015, prior to the Domestic Violence Law, stating:
Where the defendant does not subjectively have the intent to encroach upon the health of the victim or take the life of the victim, but rather seeks the victims’ physical or psychological suffering, and commits acts of abuse against them over a long period or on several occasions, gradually causing physical harm to the victim, negligently causing the victim to suffer serious injury or death; or where the victim harms or kills themselves because they are unable to stand the abuse, causing serious injury or death; it is within the scope of “causing serious harm or death to the victim” as provided for in paragraph 2 of Criminal Law article 260, and shall be convicted and punished as the crime of abuse.
3. Causation between the abuse and suicide is established by the abuser’s knowledge of the victim’s fragile state.
Case 2: Familial Rape
FACTS:
In 7/2022, Wu moved in with her mother and stepfather.
The stepfather was abusive to the mother and told Wu he had served time for homicide.
October 5, 2022- The mother fled the house.
The stepfather then recorded a video of himself having sex with Wu.
Wu wrote her mother, who called the police.
VERDICT:  Stepfather sentenced to 4 years 6 months.
Legal Principles:
Lack of resistance cannot be taken as consent in familial rape.  The case explanation notes that domestic violence is typified by control, and given the uniquely vulnerable and dependent position of the victim, lack of resistance or passive compliance is consistent with fear and cannot be viewed as voluntary consent.
Expert testimony on minors’ psychological states is permissible.  When cases involve specialised knowledge, experts can be invited- here addressing trauma to minors, neural development, etc., helping the court understand the response without bias.   Articles 146 and 197 of the  Criminal Procedure Law  allow for persons with expert subject area knowledge to inform the court about related issues.
Case 3: Delayed Reporting of Domestic Violence
F and M romantically cohabitated
5/2023, they fight at F’s workplace, with M stabbing F, threatening to kill her, and then stabbing himself.
F’s sister intervened, helped F get medical attention. The couple separates.
8/2023, M returns to Fs work, and threatens her jealously.
F calls the police, reporting previous stabbing, and M is arrested.
VERDICT:  M is sentenced to 6 years for attempted murder.
Legal Principles:
The pattern of domestic violence should be considered in determining whether the offender had the intent to kill.  Domestic violence is based on control, and when that control is threatened, the abuser may try to destroy the victim. A series of threatening texts shows that Xu feared losing control, and that this was his motive. He used very deadly means and attempted to kill himself, showing his intent was death, not injury.
Delayed reporting does not undermine credibility of the accusation.  In Domestic violence cases, victims often believe that the offender will reform and avoid reporting. Here she waited for a new threat to report, but this shouldn’t impact credibility.
CASE 4:  Evaluating Minor’s Accounts of Abuse
FACTS:
M lived with Wang’s Mother, F, since 2021.
In 2024, Wang told an aunt that M had raped her, and the aunt reported this to the police.
On May 19, police questioned Wang who recounted rapes and molestation.
On July 2, at F’s request, the police spoke with Wang again, who recanted, saying she only sought to make her mother leave M.
VERDICT:   Court believed prior 2 accounts, sentencing M to 15 years for rape and indecency.
LEGAL PRINCIPLES:
Statements from minors that contain a level of detail beyond what a non-participant would know are generally to be accepted.  Here, the level of detail, described in age-appropriate language and fashion, made it unlikely the victim was being induced, coerced, or making a false accusation. Reasons for recanting should be scrutinised, including likely intervention by the mother here.
Corroborating evidence is key to establishing the case . Chat logs with M on victims phone showed improper content; other witnesses’ verified that child had made statements consistent with accusations.
Case 5: Personal Protection Order
FACTS:
Couple married in 2008 and had a son.
During an argument, M threatened to hurt himself and pushed F when she tried to stop him.
F called the police who helped her apply for a  protection order  online. W/in 20 minutes, the court found there was Domestic Violence and issued the order, serving it to both parties.
Despite concerted education and enforcement, violence continued, and F was granted shelter while a divorce was mediated.
Legal Principles:
Threats of self-harm can be psychological violence against others.  The heart of DV is control, and threatening to harm oneself can be used to coerce a partner and constitute psychological violence.
Collaborative enforcement-  police, local organisations, health care providers, and courts all provide services and help establish a safety structure and mechanisms for issuing and enforcing a protection order.
CASE 6: Corporal Discipline can be Domestic Violence
FACTS:
F and M divorced with one daughter (D).
M was aggressive and sometimes physically punished D.
At 10 years old, he hit her mouth with a slipper, causing bleeding, and F brought her to the police
At 11 M whipped D with a belt, and again F took her to the police.
D told police that she was being hit and that she wanted to live with her mother. She was diagnosed with depression and became unable to attend school.
F sued for custody, arguing that the abuse had led to depression.
Verdict: F granted Custody
Legal Principles:
Violent discipline can be domestic violence.  Frequent physical and verbal violence violates Article 2 of the Domestic Violence Law. Pang harmed his daughter and ignored the psychological harm it was causing.
Custody decisions should consider domestic violence.  Violence poses a physical and psychological threat and is not in the best interests of the child, which is the standard for determining custody.
CASE 7
FACTS:
F and M married in 1993 and had 4 children, now grown.
F was stay at home parent and homemaker, and M was verbally and physically abusive.
In September 2019 violent abuse caused guards to intervene, and hospital records document bruises.
In May 2022, F sued for divorce with division of property, AND sought compensation for labor and damages.
LEGAL PRINCIPLES:
Fair division of property can protect women from further abuse/harassment.  Fair can mean considering practical realities as well as values, ensuring a stable livelihood. Here, the court divided storefront rental properties not only by value, but also by location, to allow F to continue renting them with minimal interaction with M.
The value of domestic labor can be evaluated at divorce.  100,000 yuan in compensation given in addition to division of property.
Case 8: Abuse can be grounds for custody reappraisal, but self-help is not proper remedy.
FACTS:
M and F married in 2022 despite a history of repeated, violent domestic violence, and had a child in the same year.
F sought help repeatedly during and after pregnancy from Womens Federation and police, and the police issued a  warning letter .
In April 2023, the couple divorced, agreeing that M would raise the child until age 4, at which point they would revisit custory.
6 months in, F discovered that M and his family were not protecting child, and removed her.
M asked court to enjoin F from infringing his custody, and the court did so.
F sued to change custody, and ultimately succeeded after presenting evidence of domestic violence.
Legal Principles:
History of domestic violence is a factor in establishing or changing child custody.  F was able to 1)explain that she had initially relinquished custody only to expedite the divorce, 2) present evidence of abuse by M, and 3) demonstrate M’s current financial instability etc.
Injunctions are appropriate where a non-custodial parent removes a child from the custodial parent’s care.  When the non-custodial parent believes the child is endangered, by the custodial parent’s gambling, drinking, or other abuse and neglect, the correct remedy is to sue for custody, not merely remove the child.
Applicable Law:
Interpretation of the Civil Code, Marriage and Family Section (2)
Article 12:  Where either the father or mother, or a person such as a close relative, takes or hides minor children, and the other party applies to the people’s court for a personal protection order, or applies for an injunction against harms to personality rights in accordance with Civil Code article 997, the people’s court is to support it.
Where the party taking or hiding minor children argues that there was a reasonable grounds for them to do so in that the other party had situations such as gambling, drug use, or domestic violence that seriously harmed the minor children’s lawful rights and interests, the people’s court shall notify them to resolve it through channels such as for revocation of guardianship rights, suspension of visitation, or changing childcare relationships. Where parties do not provide evidence in support of the arguments above and fail to submit the request in a reasonable time, the people’s court is to handle it in accordance with the preceding paragraph.
